title: Patriots 2026 position preview: Linebackers edition
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: The Patriots' linebackers excelled last season under Mike Vrabel, achieving the third-highest league grade from Pro Football Focus.
Key players like Harold Landry and Robert Spillane are expected to continue their impactful roles despite roster changes.
Rookie Gabe Jacas remains a wildcard, being the only unsigned second-round pick, offering potential depth if signed.

title: Man dead after apparent drowning in Randolph
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: A man died after an apparent drowning in Great Pond, Randolph, Sunday afternoon, according to Norfolk District Attorney's Office.
Randolph police and fire received a 911 call about a swimmer in distress around 4 p.m. near Pond Street.
State Police are investigating the incident; the man was pronounced dead at a hospital after transport by ambulance.

title: Historic Somerville Theatre and Capitol Theatre buildings hit the market
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: The Somerville Theatre and Capitol Theatre buildings are listed for sale, each with an asking price of $12 million.
Properties included in the sale are part of a three-building portfolio, with a combined market reach of $40 million.
CSB Theatres, operating the theaters, holds long-term leases despite the buildings being listed for sale.

title: Matt Turner’s loan with the Revolution extended; New England has option to buy GK from Lyon
author: Hayden Bird
bullets: New England Revolution extended Matt Turner's loan from Lyon until December 31, 2026, with an option to buy.
Turner, previously a Designated Player, is now a TAM-eligible player, impacting the Revolution's salary cap strategy.
Turner, at 32, has been pivotal for New England, helping the team reach fourth place in the Eastern Conference.

title: Trooper injured in wrong-way crash on Mass. Pike in Chicopee
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Trooper and K-9 injured by wrong-way driver on I-91 in Chicopee; injuries not life-threatening, expected to recover.
Jose G Santiago, Jr. from Holyoke charged with DUI, negligent operation, speeding after crash on Mass. Pike.
Santiago's breathalyzer reading was 0.19%, more than double the legal limit; three unopened beers found in his car.

title: Here’s how hot and humid it could get this week, according to Boston meteorologists
author: Heather Alterisio
bullets: Heat Advisory in effect from 11 AM Tuesday to 8 PM Wednesday for much of southern New England.
Temperatures will feel like 95°F-103°F with humidity on Tuesday and Wednesday, according to the National Weather Service.
Northern New England faces a stronger risk of severe thunderstorms, with potential tornadoes near the Canadian border Tuesday.

title: Red Sox used a 9th round pick in MLB Draft on an intriguing power-hitting prospect
author: Hayden Bird
bullets: Red Sox selected power-hitting outfielder Martin Shelar in the ninth round of the 2026 MLB Draft.
Shelar, 18, hit .500 with 19 home runs last year at Marist School in Georgia.
The Red Sox saved money signing first-rounder Jake Schaffner, allowing flexibility to contract Shelar.

title: A woman had to be rescued from a N.H. mountain. Then her boyfriend proposed.
author: John Waller
bullets: Alexis Hardy was rescued from Mount Kearsarge after a lower-leg injury, involving multiple emergency services.
During the rescue, Hardy's boyfriend, Tim Dupere, proposed to her, and she accepted the engagement.
After being carried down the trail, Alexis Hardy was taken to Concord Hospital for further treatment.

title: Nurses are back at Brigham and Women’s Hospital, but the fight is not over
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: The Brigham and Women's Hospital nursing strike ended, with about 4,000 nurses returning after a five-day lockout.
Negotiations continue as nurses demand higher wages, better health insurance, and investment in permanent staff.
450 Mass General Brigham Home Care nurses continue their strike, seeking contract improvements, until Wednesday.

title: These are the beaches closed in Mass. as the latest heat wave hits
author: Peter Chianca and Heather Alterisio
bullets: About 50 Massachusetts beaches are closed due to high bacteria levels and harmful algae blooms.
The Massachusetts Department of Public Health monitors bacteria levels across over 1,100 public and semi-public beaches.
State officials update the beach water quality dashboard hourly to inform the public about current beach conditions.
